content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 2, 2017

Keith White
312 Keller Street
Petaluma, CA 94952

Re: Your Request for Formal Advice
Our File No. A-17-086

Dear Mr. White:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment ("revolving door") provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the revolving door provisions of the Act and not any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

While we render formal advice with respect to a portion of your request regarding the appearance of your name on an invoice, we are treating the remainder of your request as one for informal assistance because your inquiries were not limited to specific government proceedings.

QUESTION

As a former employee of the California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC"), do the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act preclude you from working as a consultant on matters related to the 2016 Integrated Resource Plan proceeding (2016 IRP-LTPP) or on matters relating to transmission planning processes and initiatives?

CONCLUSION

The one-year ban is applicable to you and prohibits you from contracting or appearing as a paid consultant for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property before the CPUC for the one-year period. An itemized request for compensation that includes your former agency does not constitute an appearance before or communication to your former agency for purposes of Section 87406.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 2

The permanent ban is applicable to you for any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
in which you participated while working at the CPUC.

a.     Transmission Siting and Permitting Matters: For transmission siting and permitting
matters where you did not participate, in recommendations or advice, as a CPUC employee, the
permanent ban is not applicable to you, as detailed below.

b.     Modeling Methods/Transparency Recommendations: To the extent that your work
on modeling methods/transparency recommendations for the 2014 LTPP was one of general
application and did not involve specific utilities or parties, it does not meet the definition of
"proceeding" for purposes of the permanent ban and does not preclude your consultant work on the
2016 IRP-LTPP.

FACTS

Past CPUC Employment from 2005 to 2011:

You separated from state service on November 30, 2016. You were a Public Utilities
Regulatory Analyst V in the Generation and Planning Section within the Infrastructure Planning
and Siting Branch of the CPUC's Energy Division. The CPUC regulates privately owned electric
and natural gas utilities operating in California. Your position was designated in the CPUC conflict-
of-interest code, and you filed annual Statements of Economic Interest during your 11 years at the
CPUC.

Generally, a Public Utilities Regulatory Analyst V: (1) develops and implements major
studies or programs involving the coordination of several regulatory disciplines with federals,
statewide, or industrywide policy implication; (2) provides expert consultation to Commissioners
and top management in policy development on a wide range of issues relating to public utilities and
transportation regulation; (3) makes policy recommendations affecting program direction; (4) may
lead several professional subordinates and conduct workshops on the most difficult issues or direct
large major studies or regulatory programs of the broadest scope and complexity; (5) and does other
job-related work.²

Your work at the CPUC consisted of two roles.

(1) External Processes:

Early in your career you represented the CPUC in external processes administered by non-
governmental entities, such as the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, the Federal
Department of Energy, and the California Energy Commission. You also represented the CPUC at
non-profit organizations, such as the California Independent System Operator ("CAISO")³ and the
Western Electricity Coordinating Council. These external entities make decisions regarding
transmission planning, transmission cost recovery, electricity market design and operation, energy-

² https://emp.cpuc.ca.gov/sigmadocs/3UC06.pdf.

³ CAISO operates and plans the high voltage electric transmission grid servicing approximately 80% of
electricity consumption in California.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 3

related forecasting and analyses, and development and enforcement of electric reliability standards.
You state that you "were not [assigned as staff] in any CPUC proceeding."

(2) CPUC Energy Division, the Generation and Transmission Planning Section:

Commencing in 2011, you worked in the Generation and Transmission Planning Section for
"overall infrastructure planning with a principal focus on CPUC jurisdictional generation
planning."⁴ Specifically, you were tasked with the following:

1. Transmission Planning:
You were the sole member of this section focused on transmission planning.
Transmission planning regards planning of the high voltage electric grid, which utilizes
power lines and substations. This work involved analyzing and forecasting "use" and
potential insufficiencies of the transmission system under a range of possible future
conditions, including alternative market, economic, and policy scenarios. You also
examined the efficacy of potential transmission investments or operational solutions to
meet needs identified in the above analyses, taking into account economic efficiency,
electric reliability, and policy priorities.

2. Transmission Siting and Permitting Proceedings:
Certain projects would require permits from the CPUC prior to commencement. During
your employment, you provided "very occasional and brief" advice regarding "a few
transmission permitting proceedings or "concerns." You state that you do not recall the
input you provided in these matters or the details. You believe, with a few exceptions,
the proceedings you were involved in are now closed or that your involvement was of a
low level of participation. You have identified this type of proceeding as one in which
you have "a possible but unlikely" potential for a permanent ban.

3. Long Term Procurement Plan ("2014 LTPP"):
The 2014 LTPP proceeding was a large umbrella resource planning proceeding
undertaken to consider the CPUC's electric procurement policies and programs and to
ensure a safe, reliable, and cost-effective electricity supply. The proceeding evaluated
the need for, and possible authorization of, regulated utilities procurement of electric
resources in specified amounts, types, timing, and locations. The fundamental purpose of
the overall LTPP proceeding was to: model and analyze alternative credible long-term
scenarios regarding electricity demand, electric system infrastructure, and policy
requirements in order to assess the reliability, economic, and policy consequences of
those futures, and ultimately, and ultimately assess the need for and consequences of
alternative planning solutions potentially involving CPUC decisions authorizing
planning activities and investments.

⁴ Jurisdictional generation planning refers to planning for electric generation power plants through entities that
are within the CPUC regulatory jurisdiction.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 4

4. You worked on "modeling/transparency issues" for the LTPP. In this role, you made recommendations regarding certain aspects of modeling methodology used to assess and quantify the adequacy of (and need for additional) flexibility in electric system generation and storage, due to inherent operational variability and uncertainty of growing amounts of wind and solar generation. You focused your recommendations on the need for additional confidence, disclosure, and transparency regarding methods to "stochastically model" simultaneous variations and uncertainties in electricity demand, wind generation, and solar generation.5

You indicated that your modeling methods/transparency recommendations did not alter the key fundamental inputs to the LTPP proceeding. Those fundamental inputs involve characterization of alternative futures regarding electricity consumption, electric generation and transmission infrastructure, policy goals and requirements, and the operational and economic characteristics of existing and potential new electric supply solutions. Hence, you describe your "substantial contribution" to the LTPP proceeding as being limited to "a narrow technical aspect."

You provide the following list of the main recommendations to which you contributed:

a. Stochastic models have promise and value but should not be fully relied upon for CPUC decisions without further validation and confidence-building.

b. Stochastically modeled (randomly sampled from wider probability distributions of possible future conditions) combinations of electricity demand + wind generation + solar generation should specifically include, as benchmarking cases, combinations of conditions that have been documented to have occurred (co-occurring load + wind + solar conditions) in the real world.

c. Both the modeling logic (algorithms or equations) and the detailed modeling results for modeling the operation of controllable and flexible system generators (e.g., gas-fired generators) to manage/offset the variability and uncertainty of load + wind + solar conditions - - should be more fully explained and disclosed (e.g., numerical results posted) in certain specific ways described in the recommendations.

d. Greater explanation and justification were requested for the modeling methodology under which substantial amounts of flexible (e.g., gas-fired) generation were required (modeled) as needing to run at all times in certain urban load centers, to meet several kinds of reliability-based needs.

5. Integrated Resource Plan and Long Term Procurement Plan ("2016 IRP-LTPP"): Following the common two-year cycle, the 2014 LTPP proceeding was closed in June 2016. It was succeeded by the much broader 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding, R.16-02-007.

5 "Stochastically model" means that certain variables are modeled as having probability distributions rather than as single fixed values at any point in time. The key variables or conditions that were stochastically modeled included hourly electricity demand, hourly solar generation, and hourly wind generation.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 5

The 2016 IRP-LTPP is a continuation of the CPUC's biennial 2014 LTPP. The 2016 IRP-LTPP also addresses the new integrated resources planning component required by Senate Bill 350 of 2015.6

The modeling recommendations aspect of the LTPP proceeding that you worked on was not resolved before the proceeding closed. Your modeling recommendation contributions were carried forward into the 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding. You worked on the IRP proceeding from March 2016 through November 2016, mostly assisting in the transfer of certain 2014 LTPP proceeding modeling issues into the 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding and drafting attendant staff documents.

You also describe the 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding as a "quantum change" from the previous LTPP cycles because it "entails a significantly broader scope and requires considerably more comprehensive analyses." Moreover, you indicate that the modeling requirements have been "significantly refined" and the modeling challenges are "well beyond" those in the 2014 proceeding. You state that you "do not know where these modeling requirements currently stand."

6. Other Activities:
You interacted with staff/management having roles in various CPUC proceedings. The interaction often involved conveying information from external processes potentially relevant to CPUC priorities or proceedings. You also occasionally conveyed information of CPUC recommendations, comments, planning assumptions, or data to external processes. On occasion, your connection with a proceeding involved attending meetings or workshops. There were also occasions when you provided limited advice related to a proceeding by suggesting edits to documents, answering questions, or finding certain information. You believe that these various proceedings are now closed or that your participation was of a low level.

Current Post-Governmental Employment:

You are now employed at a consulting company that provides technical analysis, advice, and testimony for clients in the electric power industry. These clients include publicly owned electricity providers, such as municipal electric departments. In this position, you anticipate providing advice, analysis, and testimony related to the transmission planning processes and initiatives at the CPUC and the CAISO. Your company may contract directly with the CPUC on projects as well.

Specifically, your current employer is in discussions regarding potential consulting work for a consumer advocate, non-government organization on a CPUC transmission siting and permitting proceeding – the Southern California Edison (SCE) Alberhill System Project, A.09-09-022, and possibly the related SCE Valley-Ivyglen Subtransmission Line Project, A.07-01-031. The Alberhill System Project involves constructing a new substation, extending/rerouting a small portion of an existing transmission line to supply this proposed new substation and running new transmission

6 Senate Bill 350 of 2015 regards planning by load serving entities to meet greenhouse gas emission reduction goals, codified in CPUC §§ 8454.51 and 454.52.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 6

lines from this new substation to supply existing customer-serving substations. The Valley-Ivyglen
Subtransmission Line Project involves a new transmission line to provide a greater energy supply to
the Ivyglen substation to serve customers from the existing Valley substation.

You state that you were not involved with either of these transmission siting and permitting
proceedings during your employment with the CPUC. You further state that your work would not
involve testimony or personal communication with CPUC staff. However, the client in this matter
may request "intervenor compensation" from the CPUC for its contribution to the CPUC
proceeding. If so, your name and hours worked may appear in the client's request to the CPUC for
compensation.

You are not currently contemplating work on the 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding in any specific
capacity. However, the 2016 IRP-LTPP is a comprehensive proceeding that will continue for a
significant amount of time. The proceeding is one in which potential clients may have possible
areas of interest. You indicate that it is unclear how any possible area of interest might be related to
or influenced by the modeling recommendations that you made on the 2014 LTPP proceeding.

ANALYSIS

Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions
are commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions.¹

One-Year Ban:

The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation
18746. I.)

The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code.
(Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date
the employee permanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban
applies only when a former employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).)

In contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific
parties such as "judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance
_______________
¹ In response to your inquiry regarding the applicability of Regulation 18702.3(c), conflict of interest
provisions relating to government decisions by a public official require a separate analysis from those relating to the
post-governmental employment restrictions. Regulation 18703.3(c) is inapplicable to your request for advice. Please
contact the Commission if a situation arises where you are making, participating in making, or otherwise using your
official position to influence a governmental decision in which you have a financial interest

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 7

or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation
18746.1(b)(5).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.)

Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budget,
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(6)(C).)

We point out, however, that not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. For
example, so long as it is not otherwise prohibited by the permanent ban, a former agency official may
draft proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency, (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-
321; Harrison Advice Letter, No. A-92-289); or may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the
procedural requirements, plans, or policies of his or her former agency (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-
94-004), so long as the former employee is not identified in the client's efforts to influence the
agency.⁸ The inclusion of a former employee's name in an employer's normal letterhead does not
constitute an appearance before or communication to one's former agency for purposes of Section
87406. (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) Moreover, the inclusion of your name and hours
worked in your client's itemized request for compensation does not constitute a prohibited
appearance or communication with the CPUC because, like having your name appear on a
letterhead, it does not involve "an appearance" or "communication" for the purpose of influencing
an agency." (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.)

As a designated employee in your former state agency's conflict-of-interest code, the one-
year ban applies to you until November 30, 2017. (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(a)(2).)
Under the one-year ban you may not engage in communications or appearances to influence any
administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property. (Section 87406(d)(1); Regulation 18746.1(b).) Therefore, you may not appear or
communicate with the CPUC in any proceedings, including any permitting proceedings, on behalf
of a client or your employer. As noted above, an appearance or communication includes conversing

⁸ Regulation 18746.2(1)(b) provides some additional exceptions, as follows:

"An appearance or communication is not limited by this section when an individual: (1) Participates as a
panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research
and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; (2) Attends a general informational meeting,
seminar, or similar event; (3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or (4) Communicates with
the press."

⁹ However, please note that the permanent ban may still prohibit you from working on the proceeding as a
consultant, as discussed below.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 8

by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a
meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. You may advise clients on CPUC
requirements, plans, or policy. You may also draft proposals on a client's behalf but only to the
extent that you are not identified in any of these actions.

The same general advice applies if your employer contracts with the CPUC. You can
undertake the compensated work associated with the CPUC if you do not appear or communicate
with anyone from the CPUC divisions in any manner that could be construed as an attempt to
influence.

Permanent Ban:

The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of
absence from any office or employment. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(1).)

The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any
oral or written communication – or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in
representing any other person, other than the State of California,10 in an appearance or
communication – made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official.

"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state
administrative agency . . . ." (Section 87400(c).) Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that
affect the rights or claims of specific parties. (Lowry Advice Letter, No. I-08-053.) It does not apply
to those that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability. (Beale Advice Letter,
No. A-00-146; Chalfant Advice Letter, No. A-92-509, as cited in White Advice Letter, No. I-02-
350.) Contract procurement for generation sources, permitting transmission facilities, and
competitive bidding decisions qualify as "judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings" for
purposes of application of the permanent ban.

The Lowry letter provides examples of past advice on this issue.

"For example, we previously advised that a state administrator
official who developed a system for setting foster care rates
applicable to all group homes was not involved in a "judicial,
quasi-judicial or other proceeding" since the system did not
involve any one specific group home. (Bersinger Advice Letter,
No. A-82-209.) In addition, we advised that the formulation of a

10 The state encompasses all California state agencies, including all divisions of the CPUC. (Section 87401.)
The permanent ban is potentially applicable if you represent any other entity than the State of California.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 9

rate design policy for the Public Utilities Commission was not a
'judicial or other proceeding' because the policy had an
industrywide application and did not focus on any specific utility.
(Fong Advise Letter, No. A-88-024.)"

Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval,
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of
confidential information . . . ." (Section 87400(d).)

New Proceedings:

The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated.
(Sections 87401 and 87402; Inamine Advice Letter, No. I-11-185.) A "new" proceeding not subject
to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different
factual or legal issues from those considered in previous proceedings. This exception holds true
even when the local governmental agency client was a party to a previous proceeding in which the
former employee participated. (Puccio Advice Letter, No. I-01-079.)

Transmission Siting and Permitting Proceedings:

Your employer anticipates working on two transmission siting and permitting matters: the
Alberhill and the Valley-Ivyglen Projects. You indicate that you "were not aware" of the two
specific matters. Based on the facts you have provided, the permanent ban will not apply to either
matter. It does not apply to matters in which you did not participate "personally and substantially"
in making any recommendations or in rendering any related advice. Please review your work
history records and contact our office for additional advice on future projects, as needed.

2016 IRP-LTPP:

To the extent that your work on modeling methods/transparency recommendations for the
2014 LTPP was one of general application and did not involve specific utilities or parties, it does
not meet the definition of "proceeding" for purposes of the permanent ban, and does not preclude
your consultant work on the 2016 IRP-LTPP.

No facts indicate that the modeling recommendations you worked on involved specific
utilities or parties. Therefore your work on the 2014 LTPP does meet the definition of participating
in a "proceeding," and the permanent ban is not applicable.¹¹ As stated in Fong, and pertinent to
these facts, where a CPUC employee participated in "a phase of the rate design policy and rate
making scheme for the natural gas industry" it did not constitute participation in a "proceeding" for
revolving door purposes, because the rate design policy and rate making scheme "had an industry-
wide application and did not focus on specific utilities." Similarly, your work on modeling
methods/transparency recommendations for the 2014 LTPP, which may have carried over in some

¹¹ In response to your inquiry, Regulation 18471 1(a)(4) addresses legal advisory opinions and is not applicable
to these facts. The relevant definition of proceeding is found in Section 87400(c), as noted earlier.

File No. A-17-086
Page No. 10

form to the 2016 IRP-LTPP, were for the general purpose of evaluating available electric generation resources as a part of these "large umbrella resource planning" proceedings.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

sections.question:
As a former employee of the California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC"), do the post­
governmental employment provisions of the Act preclude you from working as a consultant on 
matters related to the 2016 Integrated Resource Plan proceeding (2016 IRP-LTPP) or on matters 
relating to transmission planning processes and initiatives?
sections.conclusion:
The one-year ban is applicable to you and prohibits you from contracting or appearing as a 
paid consultant for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property before the CPUC for the one-year 
period. An itemized request for compensation that includes your former agency does not constitute 
an appearance before or communication to your former agency for purposes of Section 87406.The permanent ban is applicable to you for any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while working at the CPUC. 
a. 
Transmission Siting and Permitting Matters: For transmission siting and permitting 
matters where you did not participate, in recommendations or advice, as a CPUC employee, the 
permanent ban is not applicable to you, as detailed below. 
b. 
Modeling Methods/Transparency Recommendations: To the extent that your work 
on modeling methods/transparency recommendations for the 2014 L TPP was one of general 
application and did not involve specific utilities or parties, it does not meet the definition of 
"proceeding" for purposes of the permanent ban and does not preclude your consultant work on the 
2016 IRP-LTPP.

and Siting Branch of the CPUC's Energy Division. The CPUC regulates privately owned electric 
and natural gas utilities operating in California. Your position was designated in the CPUC conflict­
of-interest code, and you filed annual Statements of Economic Interest during your 11 years at the

Generally, a Public Utilities Regulatory Analyst V: (l) develops and implements major 
studies or programs involving the coordination of several regulatory disciplines with federals, 
statewide, or industrywide policy implication; (2) provides expert consultation to Commissioners 
and top management in policy development on a wide range of issues relating to public utilities and

related forecasting and analyses, and development and enforcement of electric reliability standards.

3. Long Tenn Procurement Plan ("2014 L TPP"):

4. You worked on "modeling/transparency issues" for the LTPP. In this role, you made

You indicated that your modeling methods/transparency recommendations did not alter

occurring load+ wind+ solar conditions) in the real world. 
c. Both the modeling logic (algorithms or equations) and the detailed modeling 
results for modeling the operation of controllable and flexible system generators 
(e.g., gas-fired generators) to manage/offset the variability and uncertainty of 
load +wind+ solar conditions - - should be more fully explained and disclosed 
(e.g., numerical results posted) in certain specific ways described in the 
recommendations. 
d. Greater explanation and justification were requested for the modeling 
methodology under which substantial amounts of flexible (e.g., gas-fired) 
generation were required (modeled) as needing to run at all times in certain urban 
load centers, to meet several kinds of reliability-based needs. 
5. Integrated Resource Plan and Long Term Procurement Plan {"2016 IRP-LTPP"): 
Following the common two-year cycle, the 2014 LTPP proceeding was closed in June 
2016. It was succeeded by the much broader 2016 IRP-L TPP proceeding, R.16-02-007. 
5 "Stochastically model" means that certain variables are modeled as having probability distributions rather 
than as single fixed values at any point in time. The key variables or conditions that were stochastically modeled 
included hourly electricity demand, hourly solar generation, and hourly wind generation.  
The 2016 IRP-LTPP is a continuation of the CPUC's biennial 2014 LTPP. The 2016 
IRP-L TPP also addresses the new integrated resources planning component required by 
Senate Bill 350 of2015.6 
The modeling recommendations aspect of the L TPP proceeding that you worked on was 
not resolved before the proceeding closed. Your modeling recommendation 
contributions were carried forward into the 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding. You worked on 
the IRP proceeding from March 2016 through November 2016, mostly assisting in the 
transfer of certain 2014 L TPP proceeding modeling issues into the 2016 IRP-L TPP 
proceeding and drafting attendant staff documents. 
You also describe the 2016 IRP-L TPP proceeding as a "quantum change" from the 
previous LTPP cycles because it "entails a significantly broader scope and requires 
considerably more comprehensive analyses." Moreover, you indicate that the modeling 
requirements have been "significantly refined" and the modeling challenges are "well 
beyond" those in the 2014 proceeding. You state that you "do not know where these 
modeling requirements curr_ently stand." 
6. Other Activities: 
You interacted with staff/management having roles in various CPUC proceedings. The 
interaction often involved conveying information from external processes potentially 
relevant to CPUC priorities or proceedings. You also occasionally conveyed infonnation 
of CPUC recommendations, comments, planning assumptions, or data to external 
processes. On occasion, your connection with a proceeding involved attending meetings 
or workshops. There were also occasions when you provided limited advice related to a 
proceeding by suggesting edits to documents, answering questions, or finding certain 
information. You believe that these various proceedings are now closed or that your 
participation was of a low level. 
Current Post-Governmental Employment: 
You are now employed at a consulting company that provides technical analysis, advice, 
and testimony for clients in the electric power industry. These clients include publicly owned 
electricity providers, such as municipal electric departments. In this position, you anticipate 
providing advice, analysis, and testimony related to the transmission planning processes and 
initiatives at the CPUC and the CAISO. Your company may contract directly with the CPUC on 
projects as well. 
Specifically, your current employer is in discussions regarding potential consulting work for 
a consumer advocate, non-government organization on a CPUC transmission siting and pennitting 
proceeding - the Southern California Edison (SCE) Alberhill System Project, A.09-09-022, and 
possibly the related SCE Valley-Ivyglen Subtransmission Line Project, A.07-01-031. The Alberhill 
System Project involves constructing a new substation, extending/rerouting a small portion of an 
existing transmission line to supply this proposed new substation and running new transmission 
<, Senate Bill 350 of 2015 regards planning by load serving entities to meet greenhouse gas emission reduction 
goals, codified in CPUC §§ 8454.51 and 454.52.  
lines from this new substation to supply existing customer-serving substations. The Valley-lvyglen 
Subtransmission Line Project involves a new transmission line to provide a greater energy supply to 
the lvyglen substation to serve customers from the existing Valley substation. 
You state that you were not involved with either of these transmission siting and pennitting 
proceedings during your employment with the CPUC. You further state that your work would not 
involve testimony or personal communication with CPUC staff. However, the client in this matter 
may request .. intervenor compensation" from the CPUC for its contribution to the CPUC 
proceeding. If so, your name and hours worked may appear in the client's request to the CPUC for 
compensation. 
You are not currently contemplating work on the 2016 IRP-LTPP proceeding in any specific 
capacity. However, the 2016 IRP-LTPP is a comprehensive proceeding that will continue for a 
significant amount of time. The proceeding is one in which potential clients may have possible 
areas of interest. You indicate that it is unclear how any possible area of interest might be related to 
or influenced by the modeling recommendations that you made on the 2014 LTPP proceeding.
sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. These provisions 
are. commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions.7 
One-Year Ban: 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746. l .) 
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a 
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
(Section 87406( d)( I); Regulation I 8746. l (a)(2).) The ban applies for twelve months from the date 
the employee pennanently leaves state office or employment. While in effect, the one-year ban 
applies only when a fonner employee or official is being compensated for his or her appearances or 
communications before his or her former agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or 
representative of that person. (Regulation 18746.l(b)(3) and (4).) 
In contrast to the pennanent ban, which only applies to certain matters involving specific 
parties such as .. judicial or quasi-judicial" proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance 
7 In response to your inquiry regarding the applicability of Regulation 18702.3( c ), conflict of interest 
provisions relating to government decisions by a public official require a separate analysis from those relating to the 
post-governmental employment restrictions. Regulation 18702.3(c) is inapplicable to your request for advice. Please 
contact the Commission if a situation arises where you are making, participating in making, or otherwise using your 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which you have a financial interest  
or communication made for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or 
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation 
of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 
18746.l (b)(S).) An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" ifit is made 
for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication 
includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by 
electronic transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Id.) 
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (1) before a state 
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state agency "which budget, 
personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state agency the public official 
worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to the direction and control of the 
Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office during the twelve 
months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation 187 46.1 (b )( 6)(C).) 
We point out, however, that not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. For 
example, so long as not otherwise prohibited by the Ýpermanent ban, a former agency official may 
draft proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency. ( Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-
321; Harrison Advice Letter, No. A-92-289); or may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the 
procedural requirements, plans, or policies of his or her former agency (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-
94-004), so long as the former employee is not identified in the client's efforts to influence the 
agency. 8 The inclusion of a former employee's name in an employer's normal letterhead does not 
constitute an appearance before or communication to one's former agency for purposes of Section 
87406. (Peny Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) Moreover, the inclusion of your name and hours 
worked in your client's itemized request for compensation does not constitute a prohibited 
appearance or communication with the CPUC because, like having your name appear on a 
letterhead, it does not involve "an appearance" or "communication" for the purpose of influencing 
an agency.9 (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) 
As a designated employee in your former state agency's conflict-of-interest code, the one­
year ban applies to you until November 30, 2017. (Section 87406(d)(l ); Regulation l 8746.l(a)(2).) 
Under the one-year ban you may not engage in communications or appearances to influence any 
administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or 
property. (Section 87406(d)(l); Regulation 18746. I(b).) Therefore, you may not appear or 
communicate with the CPUC in any proceedings, including any permitting proceedings, on behalf 
of a client or your employer. As noted above, an appearance or communication includes conversing 
8 Regulation 18746.2 ( 1 )(b) provides some additional exceptions, as follows: 
"An appearance or communication is not limited by this section when an individual: (l) Participates as a 
panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research 
and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; (2) Attends a general infonnational meeting, 
seminar, or similar event; (3) Requests informatioh concerning any matter of public record; or (4) Communicates with 
the press." 
 However, please note that the pennanent ban may still prohibit you from working on the proceeding as a 
consultant, as discussed below.  
by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, attending a 
meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. You may advise clients on CPUC 
requirements, plans, or policy. You may also draft proposals on a client's behalf but only to the 
extent that you are not identified in any of these actions. 
The same general advice applies if your employer contracts with the CPUC. You can 
undertake the compensated work associated with the CPUC if you do not appear or communicate 
with anyone from the CPUC divisions in any manner that could be construed as an attempt to 
influence. 
Permanent Ban: 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) The permanent ban applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of 
absence from any office or employment. (Regulation 18741. l(a)(l ).) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, 10 in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency . . .. " (Section 87400(c).) Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that 
affect the rights or claims of specific parties. (Low,y Advice Letter, No. 1-08-053.) It does not apply 
to those that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability. (Beale Advice Letter, 
No. A-00-146; Chalfant Advice Letter, No. A-92-509, as cited in White Advice Letter, No. 1-02-
350.) Contract procurement for generation sources, permitting transmission facilities, and 
competitive bidding decisions qualify as "judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceedings" for 
purposes of application of the permanent ban. 
The Low,y letter provides examples of past advice on this issue. 
"For example, we previously advised that a state administrator 
official who developed a system for setting foster care rates 
applicable to all group homes was not involved in a "judicial, 
quasi-judicial or other proceeding'' since the system did not 
involve any one specific group home. (Bersinger Advice Letter, 
No. A-82-209.) In addition, we advised that the formulation of a 
'° The state encompasses all California state agencies, including all divisions of the CPUC. (Section 8740 l .) 
The pennanent ban is potentially applicable if you represent any other entity than the State of California. 


rate design policy for the Public Utilities Commission was not a 
'judicial or other proceeding' because the policy had an 
industrywide application and did not focus on any specific utility. 
(Fong Advise Letter, No. A-88-024.)"  
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she 
took part in the proceeding "'personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, 
formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of 
confidential information . . .. " (Section 87400(d).) 
New Proceedings: 
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
(Sections 87401 and 87402; /11amine Advice Letter, No. 1-11-185.) A "new" proceeding not subject 
to the permanent ban typically involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different 
factual or legal issues from those considered in previous proceedings. This exception holds true 
even when the local governmental agency client was a party to a previous proceeding in which the 
former employee participated. (Puccio Advice Letter, No. 1-01-079.) 
Transmission Siting and Permitting Proceedings: 
Your employer anticipates working on two transmission siting and permitting matters: the 
Alberhill and the Valley-Ivyglen Projects. You indicate that you "were not aware" of the two 
specific matters. Based on the facts you have provided, the permanent ban will not apply to either 
matter. It does not apply to matters in which you did not participate "personally and substantially" 
in making any recommendations or in rendering any related advice. Please review your work 
history records and contact our office for additional advice on future projects, as needed. 
2016 IRP-LTPP: 
To the extent that your work on modeling methods/transparency recommendations for the 
2014 LTPP was one of general application and did not involve specific utilities or parties, it does 
not meet the definition of "proceeding" for purposes of the permanent ban, and does not preclude 
your consultant work on the 2016 IRP-LTPP 
No facts indicate that the modeling recommendations you worked on involved specific 
utilities or parties. Therefore your work on the 201 4  LTPP does meet the definition of participating 
in a "proceeding," and the permanent ban is not applicable. 1 1  As stated in Fong, and pertinent to 
these facts, where a CPUC employee participated in "a phase of the rate design policy and rate 
making scheme for the natural gas industry" it did not constitute participation in a "proceeding" for 
revolving door purposes, because the rate design policy and rate making scheme "had an industry­
wide application and did not focus on specific utilities." Similarly, your work on modeling 
methods/transparency recommendations for the 2014 L TPP, which may have carried over in some 
11 In response to your inquiry, Regulation I 847 I. I (a)(4) addresses legal advisory opinions and is not applicable 
to these facts. The relevant definition of proceeding is found in Section 87400(c), as noted earlier.  
fonn to the 2016 IRP-L TPP, were for the general purpose of evaluating available electric generation 
resources as a part of these "large umbrella resource planning" proceedings.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a former employee of the California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC"), do the post­
governmental employment provisions of the Act preclude you from working as a consultant on 
matters related to the 2016 Integrated Resource Plan proceeding (2016 IRP-LTPP) or on matters 
relating to transmission planning processes and initiatives?

CONCLUSION: The one-year ban is applicable to you and prohibits you from contracting or appearing as a 
paid consultant for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property before the CPUC for the one-year 
period. An itemized request for compensation that includes your former agency does not constitute 
an appearance before or communication to your former agency for purposes of Section 87406.The permanent ban is applicable to you for any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while working at the CPUC. 
a. 
Transmission Siting and Permitting Matters: For transmission siting and permitting 
matters where you did not participate, in recommendations or advice, as a CPUC employee, the 
permanent ban is not applicable to you, as detailed below. 
b. 
Modeling Methods/Transparency Recommendations: To the extent that your work 
on modeling methods/transparency recommendations for the 2014 L TPP was one of general 
application and did not involve specific utilities or parties, it does not meet the definition of 
"proceeding" for purposes of the permanent ban and does not preclude your consultant work on the 
2016 IRP-LTPP.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 2, 2017 Keith White 312 Keller Street Petaluma, CA 94952 Re: Your Request for Formal Advice Our File No. A-17-086 Dear Mr. White: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment ("revolving door") provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the revolving door provisions of the Act and not any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. While we render formal advice with respect to a portion of your request regarding the appearance of your name on an invoice, we are treating the remainder of your request as one for informal assistance because your inquiries were not limited to specific government proceedings. QUESTION As a former employee of the California Public Utilities Commission ("CPUC"), do the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act preclude you from working as a consultant on matters related to the 2016 Integrated Resource Plan proceeding (2016 IRP-LTPP) or on matters relating to transmission planning processes and initiatives? CONCLUSION The one-year ban is applicable to you and prohibits you from contracting or appearing as a paid consultant for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property before the CPUC for the one-year period. An itemized request for compensation that includes your former agency does not constitute an appearance before or communication to your former agency for purposes of Section 87406. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-086 Page No. 2 The permanent ban is applicable to you for any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which you participated while working at the CPUC. a. Transmission Siting and Permitting Matters: For transmission siting and permitting matters where you did not participate, in recommendations or advice, as a CPUC employee, the permanent ban is not applicable to you, as detailed below. b. Modeling Methods/Transparency Recommendations: To the extent that your work on modeling methods/transparency recommendations for the 2014 LTPP was one of general application and did not involve specific utilities or parties, it does not meet the definition of "proceeding" for purposes of the permanent ban and does not preclude your consultant
